posts.text:
Organizations aren't unified entities—they're arenas of competing stakeholder goals and hidden factions.

Our new paper in Management Science introduces Subcoalition Cluster Analysis (SCA) to uncover invisible coalitions driving conflict 🧵

https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/

Most management models treat organizations as monolithic. Reality? Teams form subcoalitions with aligned preferences.

These fault lines drive decision-making chaos—but they're hard to observe and even harder to model with traditional methods.

SCA works like a smarter, preference-based clustering algorithm. It groups organizational actors into subcoalitions by maximizing shared utility.

Result? A clear map of internal divisions, even with sparse or messy data 📊

Unlike traditional clustering methods that assume external factors drive preferences, SCA flips the script:

✅ Coalitions form organically from shared goals
✅ Bottom-up, relational approach
✅ Built for messy real-world data

Case study: Wikipedia's 2013 Visual Editor rollout sparked rebellion from veteran editors.

SCA revealed two warring subcoalitions:
🛠️ Technical design focus
🌍 Cultural norms focus

Leadership missed these divisions—leading to strategy disaster

Baseball Hall of Fame case (2009-2016): Writers debated inducting players linked to PEDs.

SCA uncovered a growing rift:
⚾ Pro-PED forgiveness coalition
⚾ Anti-PED purist coalition

This schism reinforced decision-making silos over time

SCA turns organizational chaos into clarity. Managers can:

✅ Identify & anticipate internal conflicts
✅ Tailor strategies to real divisions, not assumptions
✅ Avoid paralysis from hidden factional wars

💻 Handles incomplete data across industries

Whether you're a researcher, manager, or governance practitioner studying organizational conflict, SCA offers new insights into how coalitions form and evolve.

Paper: https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/10.1287/mnsc.2020.00013

@GRAIL_center @purduepolsci [Add coauthors]

#AI

linkedin.text:
Organizations aren't unified entities—they're arenas of competing stakeholder goals and hidden factions. Our new research in Management Science introduces Subcoalition Cluster Analysis (SCA), a computational method to uncover the invisible coalitions driving organizational conflict. https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/10.1287/mnsc.2020.00013

Most management models treat organizations as monolithic, but reality is messier. Teams form subcoalitions with aligned preferences, creating fault lines that drive decision-making chaos. These divisions are hard to observe and even harder to model with traditional methods.

➤ SCA works like a smarter, preference-based clustering algorithm, grouping organizational actors by maximizing shared utility
➤ Unlike traditional methods that assume external factors drive preferences, SCA shows how coalitions form organically from shared goals
➤ Built for messy real-world data with incomplete information

Two compelling case studies demonstrate SCA's power:

• Wikipedia's 2013 Visual Editor rollout sparked rebellion from veteran editors. SCA revealed two warring subcoalitions—one focused on technical design, another on cultural norms. Leadership missed these divisions, leading to strategic disaster.

• Baseball Hall of Fame voting (2009-2016) on players linked to PEDs. SCA uncovered a growing rift between pro-PED forgiveness and anti-PED purist coalitions, showing how schisms reinforced decision-making silos over time.

For managers and governance practitioners, SCA offers practical benefits: identify and anticipate internal conflicts, tailor strategies to real divisions rather than assumptions, and avoid paralysis from hidden factional wars.

[Add coauthors] | [Add funder]

GRAIL Center | Purdue University

#AIGovernance #ResponsibleAI #OrganizationalBehavior #DataScience #ConflictResolution #TechPolicy
